repository.name: 0x12th-playbooks
repository.description: Practical playbooks and agent skills for product decisions, architecture, delivery, migration planning, technical decision-making, and software evolution.

auto_selection.manifest_role: This manifest is an index and documentation aid for available skills, use cases, non-use cases, installation hints, and selection examples.
limitations: Different AI coding agents may not read this manifest automatically.
This manifest does not define an official cross-agent standard.
Skill auto-selection should not depend on this manifest being loaded.
auto_selection.recommended_installation: Install complete skill folders so agents can access SKILL.md plus supporting docs, templates, and examples after selection.

notes: Replace ~/.agents/skills with the skills directory used by your agent setup.
Clone-based installation is recommended because skills include supporting docs, templates, and examples.
Raw SKILL.md URL imports may omit supporting files depending on the host agent.

skills.name: product-evolution
skills.purpose: Answer what the highest-value product investment is.
selection_hints: Use when the primary question is whether, why, when, for whom, or in what MVP scope to invest.
Use for obvious product prompts such as should we build this, is this worth doing, what should come first, MVP, roadmap priority, customer request, and feature scope.
Use Priority Arbitration when competing initiatives cannot all receive resources now.
After the product decision is made, hand technical design and readiness questions to engineering-architecture and implementation to engineering-delivery.

skills.name: engineering-architecture
skills.purpose: Answer how the system should evolve safely.
non_use_cases: code review of diffs or PRs
selection_hints: Use when the request asks whether, why, where, or how the system should evolve technically.

Use when the user asks for architecture assessment, decision support, migration review, production readiness, deployment readiness, release readiness, operational readiness, runtime resource review, VPS/server fit, current architecture, target architecture, or capacity and scaling review.
Use Deployment Readiness Review for prompts such as is it ready for production, can I deploy this to a VPS, is it ready for an update, review deployment readiness, or will this fit on this server.
Use Technical Evolution when a repository-wide or project-wide review asks about scaling, migration, technical sequencing, or architecture evolution.
Do not use for product investment decisions, MVP scope, customer request value, or priority arbitration.
Do not use when the user asks to implement code directly.

skills.name: engineering-delivery
skills.purpose: Answer the safest next delivery action, defaulting to read-only diagnosis unless changes are explicitly requested.
selection_hints: Use read-only diagnosis for prompts like why is this happening, where is the problem, analyze this error, what changed, or review selected code/logs/errors/diffs/files/failures.
Edit only when the user explicitly asks to implement, fix, patch, modify, update, refactor, or apply changes.
For runtime failures, follow the traceback before repository exploration.
If product or architecture decisions are required, state the missing decision before delivery work continues.

Do not begin delivery investigation for production readiness, deployment readiness, server/VPS fit, runtime resource review, current architecture, target architecture, capacity, scaling, or repository-wide technical review prompts; those require engineering-architecture first.
Stay close to selected code or explicitly named files.

selection_examples.request: What is the smallest useful solution?
A customer asked for X. Should we do it?
Should we merge two tightly coupled modules?
How should we start the merge step by step?
Can I deploy this to a VPS?
What should the target architecture be?
Move the task handler to the already approved queue runtime.
Review this PR for bugs and missing tests.

global_principles: use explicit scope and stop conditions
optional memory never replaces current evidence
product decisions before architecture before delivery
